title: Credit / Cost per Query
spl:
index=datawarehouse sourcetype="snowflake:query_history"
| eval cost=CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES * 3
| stats sum(cost) as total_cost, count as query_count by USER_NAME, WAREHOUSE_NAME
| eval cost_per_query=round(total_cost/query_count,2)
| sort -total_cost
description: Directly ties compute cost to individual queries, enabling chargeback and cost optimization. Identifies runaway queries consuming excessive resources.
value: Directly ties compute cost to individual queries, enabling chargeback and cost optimization. Identifies runaway queries consuming excessive resources.
implementation: Poll query history with cost metrics daily. Calculate cost per query, per user, and per team (using role mapping). Create weekly cost report. Alert on individual queries exceeding cost threshold. Set up warehouse-level budgets.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom API input (Snowflake ACCOUNT_USAGE).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: Snowflake `QUERY_HISTORY` (CREDITS_USED), BigQuery `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS` (total_bytes_billed).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Poll query history with cost metrics daily. Calculate cost per query, per user, and per team (using role mapping). Create weekly cost report. Alert on individual queries exceeding cost threshold. Set up warehouse-level budgets.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=datawarehouse sourcetype="snowflake:query_history"
| eval cost=CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES * 3
| stats sum(cost) as total_cost, count as query_count by USER_NAME, WAREHOUSE_NAME
| eval cost_per_query=round(total_cost/query_count,2)
| sort -total_cost
```

Understanding this SPL

**Credit / Cost per Query** ‚Äî Directly ties compute cost to individual queries, enabling chargeback and cost optimization. Identifies runaway queries consuming excessive resources.

Documented **Data sources**: Snowflake `QUERY_HISTORY` (CREDITS_USED), BigQuery `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS` (total_bytes_billed). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom API input (Snowflake ACCOUNT_USAGE). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: datawarehouse; **sourcetype**: snowflake:query_history. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=datawarehouse, sourcetype="snowflake:query_history". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **cost** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by USER_NAME, WAREHOUSE_NAME** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **cost_per_query** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (cost by user/warehouse), Table (most expensive queries), Line chart (daily cost trend), Pie chart (cost by team).
visualization: Bar chart (cost by user/warehouse), Table (most expensive queries), Line chart (daily cost trend), Pie chart (cost by team).
